questions.question: What is a deadlock in operating systems?
questions.correct_answer: A state where two or more processes each wait for the other to release a resource
incorrect_answers: A program that crashes due to a null pointer

questions.question: What is polymorphism in OOP?
questions.correct_answer: The ability of different objects to respond to the same interface in different ways
incorrect_answers: The process of hiding internal implementation

The practice of writing code once and reusing it

questions.correct_answer: A mechanism where one class acquires properties and methods from another
incorrect_answers: Bundling data and methods into a single unit
The ability to redefine a method in a subclass
Creating multiple instances of a class

questions.question: What is the difference between the stack and the heap in memory?
questions.correct_answer: Stack is used for static allocation; heap is used for dynamic allocation
incorrect_answers: Heap is used for function call frames

questions.question: What is Big O notation used for?

incorrect_answers: A sorted collection stored in contiguous memory

questions.question: What is a foreign key in a relational database?

questions.question: What is the key difference between TCP and UDP?

questions.question: What does MVC stand for in software architecture?
questions.correct_answer: Model-View-Controller

questions.question: What is the average-case time complexity of quicksort?
questions.correct_answer: O(n log n)

incorrect_answers: Allowing a class to inherit from multiple parents

questions.question: What is a pointer in C?
questions.correct_answer: A variable that stores the memory address of another variable
incorrect_answers: A variable that stores a boolean value

questions.question: What is the purpose of a garbage collector?
questions.correct_answer: To automatically reclaim memory that is no longer in use

questions.question: What is a race condition?

incorrect_answers: A performance bottleneck in the CPU

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?

incorrect_answers: A thread owns its own memory; a process shares memory

A process is faster than a thread

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 404 mean?

questions.question: What is a closure in programming?

incorrect_answers: A function with no return value

questions.question: What is memoization?
questions.correct_answer: Caching the results of expensive function calls so repeated calls return the cached result

questions.question: What is virtual memory?
questions.correct_answer: A memory management technique that uses disk space to simulate additional RAM

questions.question: What is the difference between a compiled and an interpreted language?
questions.correct_answer: Compiled languages are translated entirely to machine code before running; interpreted ones are translated line by line at runtime
incorrect_answers: Compiled languages run slower than interpreted ones

questions.question: What is a regular expression?

incorrect_answers: Redefining a parent class method in a subclass

questions.question: What does the CAP theorem state for distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance
incorrect_answers: All distributed systems must be consistent and available
Consistency and availability are mutually exclusive

questions.question: What is the purpose of an index in a database?
questions.correct_answer: To speed up data retrieval by allowing faster lookups
incorrect_answers: To enforce unique constraints on a table

questions.question: What is a callback function?

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'try-catch' blocks?

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of merge sort?

incorrect_answers: DFS is always faster than BFS

questions.question: What is immutability in programming?

questions.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous programming?

incorrect_answers: Synchronous code runs on multiple threads

questions.question: What is an enum in programming?

questions.question: What is duck typing?

questions.question: What is event-driven programming?

questions.question: What is a decorator in Python?

questions.question: What is a REST API?

incorrect_answers: An API that only works over WebSockets

An API that requires authentication tokens only

questions.question: What is a binary tree?

incorrect_answers: A tree stored as binary data on disk
A tree with exactly two levels
A sorted list of binary numbers

questions.question: What is the purpose of the SSL/TLS protocol?

questions.question: What is a microservices architecture?
questions.correct_answer: An approach where an application is built as a collection of small, independently deployable services
incorrect_answers: An architecture with a single shared database for all services

questions.question: What does a CDN (Content Delivery Network) do?

questions.question: What is the purpose of Docker?

questions.question: What is tail recursion?

incorrect_answers: A recursion that always reaches its base case immediately
Recursion that starts from the end of a list

questions.question: What is continuous integration (CI)?

incorrect_answers: Manually deploying code to production every week

questions.question: What is a JWT (JSON Web Token) used for?

questions.question: What is the difference between '==' and 'is' in Python?

questions.question: What is a doubly linked list?

questions.question: What is the purpose of code refactoring?

questions.question: What is a semaphore in operating systems?

questions.question: What is a buffer overflow?

A UI bug caused by too much text

incorrect_answers: A pattern that logs all function calls

A pattern that caches expensive computations

questions.question: What is the difference between a deep copy and a shallow copy?

incorrect_answers: A shallow copy is always slower
A deep copy and shallow copy are identical for primitive types and objects
A shallow copy creates a new object with all new data

questions.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm used for?

incorrect_answers: Searching for a value in a binary tree
Detecting cycles in a directed graph

questions.question: What is a namespace in programming?

incorrect_answers: The memory address of a variable
A protocol for naming network devices

questions.question: What is static memory allocation?

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 500 indicate?
questions.correct_answer: Internal Server Error — something went wrong on the server

questions.question: What is the Factory design pattern?

incorrect_answers: A pattern that restricts a class to one instance

questions.question: What is an abstract class?

incorrect_answers: A class that can only have static methods
A class that is automatically deleted after use

questions.question: What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class?

incorrect_answers: They are identical concepts in all languages

An abstract class cannot be inherited

questions.question: What is a priority queue?

incorrect_answers: A queue that processes elements alphabetically
A queue limited to a fixed number of elements
A queue that removes the oldest element first

questions.question: What data structure is commonly used to implement a priority queue?

questions.question: What is the difference between a min-heap and a max-heap?
questions.correct_answer: In a min-heap the smallest element is at the root; in a max-heap the largest element is at the root
incorrect_answers: A min-heap is always smaller in size

questions.question: What is a trie (prefix tree)?
questions.correct_answer: A tree-like data structure used to efficiently store and search strings by their prefixes
incorrect_answers: A binary tree used for sorting

A graph with three nodes per level

questions.question: What is dynamic programming?

incorrect_answers: A method for dynamically allocating memory

An approach to writing self-modifying programs

questions.question: What is the difference between pass by value and pass by reference?

incorrect_answers: They are the same in all programming languages
Pass by reference always creates a copy
Pass by value is only used for objects

questions.question: What is a hash collision?

incorrect_answers: When a hash table runs out of space
When two hash tables have the same size
When a hash function produces a negative number

questions.question: What is the difference between GET and POST HTTP methods?

incorrect_answers: GET is more secure than POST
POST can only send text data

questions.question: What are the three main types of tree traversal for binary trees?
questions.correct_answer: In-order, pre-order, and post-order

questions.question: In an in-order traversal of a binary search tree, what order are nodes visited?
questions.correct_answer: Left subtree, root, right subtree — producing a sorted sequence

questions.question: What is the difference between NoSQL and SQL databases?
questions.correct_answer: SQL databases use structured tables with fixed schemas; NoSQL databases use flexible schemas like documents, key-value pairs, or graphs
incorrect_answers: NoSQL is always faster than SQL

questions.question: What is an adjacency list in graph representation?

incorrect_answers: A sorted list of all edges

questions.question: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?

incorrect_answers: Parallelism is a subset of concurrency with no distinction
Concurrency requires multiple CPUs; parallelism does not

questions.question: What is a memory leak?
questions.correct_answer: When a program allocates memory but fails to release it after it is no longer needed, causing increasing memory consumption
incorrect_answers: When memory is corrupted by a virus
When a program uses too much CPU

questions.question: What is an event loop?
questions.correct_answer: A programming construct that waits for and dispatches events or messages in a program, commonly used in asynchronous I/O
incorrect_answers: A for loop that runs forever
A loop that generates random events
A hardware component in the CPU

questions.question: What is type casting?
questions.correct_answer: Converting a value from one data type to another
incorrect_answers: Assigning a value to a variable
Checking a variable's type at runtime

questions.question: What is the Strategy design pattern?
questions.correct_answer: A pattern that defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable at runtime
incorrect_answers: A pattern that ensures only one instance exists
A pattern that chains objects to handle a request
A pattern for lazy initialization of objects

questions.question: What is middleware in web development?
questions.correct_answer: Software that sits between a request and a response, processing or modifying the request before it reaches the handler
incorrect_answers: The front-end framework used to build UI
The database layer of an application
The CSS that styles a webpage

questions.question: What is a WebSocket?
questions.correct_answer: A protocol that provides full-duplex, persistent communication between a client and server over a single TCP connection

questions.question: What is the difference between unit testing and integration testing?
questions.correct_answer: Unit testing tests individual components in isolation; integration testing tests how components work together
incorrect_answers: Unit testing tests the full application; integration testing tests single functions
They are different names for the same thing
Integration testing is always automated; unit testing is manual

questions.question: What is a generics (or template) in programming?
questions.correct_answer: A feature that allows writing code that works with any data type, specified at compile time or instantiation
incorrect_answers: A function that generates random numbers
A class that can only store strings
A design pattern for creating objects

questions.question: What is OAuth?
questions.correct_answer: An open standard for token-based authorization that allows third-party access to user resources without sharing credentials

questions.question: What is GraphQL?
questions.correct_answer: A query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data they need in a single request

questions.question: What is rate limiting?
questions.correct_answer: A technique to control the number of requests a client can make to a server within a given time period
incorrect_answers: Limiting the size of API responses
Restricting the number of database tables

questions.question: What is an idempotent operation?
questions.correct_answer: An operation that produces the same result no matter how many times it is performed
incorrect_answers: An operation that runs only once
An operation that always fails on retry
An operation that changes state every time

questions.question: What is a circular buffer (ring buffer)?
questions.correct_answer: A fixed-size buffer that wraps around to the beginning when it reaches the end, overwriting old data
incorrect_answers: A buffer shaped like a circle in memory
A linked list that loops forever

questions.question: What is the Adapter design pattern?
questions.correct_answer: A structural pattern that converts the interface of a class into another interface that a client expects
incorrect_answers: A pattern for connecting to databases
A pattern for creating network adapters
A pattern that splits a class into smaller parts

questions.question: What is a topological sort?
questions.correct_answer: A linear ordering of vertices in a directed acyclic graph such that every directed edge u→v has u before v
incorrect_answers: Sorting elements by their geographic location
Sorting nodes by their depth in a tree
Sorting a list of numbers in descending order

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 401 mean?
questions.correct_answer: Unauthorized — the request requires authentication

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 403 mean?
questions.correct_answer: Forbidden — the server understood the request but refuses to authorize it

questions.question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
questions.correct_answer: Horizontal scaling adds more machines; vertical scaling adds more resources to an existing machine

questions.question: What is a binary search tree (BST)?
questions.correct_answer: A binary tree where the left child is less than the parent and the right child is greater
incorrect_answers: A tree that stores only binary data
A tree with exactly two levels
A tree where all leaves have the same value

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting into a balanced BST?
questions.correct_answer: O(log n)

questions.question: What is a graph cycle?
questions.correct_answer: A path in a graph that starts and ends at the same vertex without repeating edges
incorrect_answers: A graph with exactly one edge
A loop in a programming language

questions.question: What is functional programming?
questions.correct_answer: A paradigm that treats computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, emphasizing immutability and avoiding side effects
incorrect_answers: Programming that only uses functions, not classes
Programming that runs faster than OOP
A style that requires no variables

questions.question: What is a higher-order function?
questions.correct_answer: A function that takes other functions as arguments or returns a function as its result
incorrect_answers: A function with more than five parameters
A function defined inside another function
A function that runs with elevated privileges

questions.question: What is a message queue in distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: A component that stores messages between services, allowing asynchronous communication and decoupling
incorrect_answers: A priority queue for CPU scheduling
A data structure for sorting messages alphabetically
A buffer for storing email drafts

questions.question: What is the purpose of a reverse proxy?
questions.correct_answer: To sit in front of backend servers, forwarding client requests and providing load balancing, caching, or security
incorrect_answers: To reverse the direction of network packets

questions.question: What is paging in operating systems?
questions.correct_answer: A memory management scheme that divides virtual memory into fixed-size blocks called pages, mapped to physical frames

questions.question: What is a page fault?
questions.correct_answer: An interrupt that occurs when a program accesses a memory page not currently in physical RAM, requiring it to be loaded from disk
incorrect_answers: An error in a web page's HTML
A bug in the page numbering of a document
A failure in the CPU's paging unit

questions.question: What is Agile software development?
questions.correct_answer: An iterative approach to software delivery that builds software in increments with continuous feedback and adaptation
incorrect_answers: A programming language for web development
A single long phase of development followed by testing

questions.question: What is a Scrum sprint?
questions.correct_answer: A fixed time period (usually 2-4 weeks) during which a team completes a set of planned work items

questions.question: What is the purpose of hashing a password before storing it?
questions.correct_answer: To convert the password into a fixed-length irreversible string so the original password cannot be recovered if the database is compromised
incorrect_answers: To compress the password to save storage space
To encrypt the password so it can be decrypted later

questions.question: What is an IP subnet?
questions.correct_answer: A logical subdivision of an IP network that groups devices and controls traffic routing

questions.question: What does CIDR notation (e.g., /24) represent?
questions.correct_answer: The number of bits in the network prefix of an IP address, defining the subnet size
incorrect_answers: The number of devices on a network
The speed of the network in Mbps

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer?
questions.correct_answer: A pointer that references a memory location that has been freed or deallocated
incorrect_answers: A pointer with no assigned value
A pointer that points to the stack
A pointer used in a linked list

questions.question: What is the difference between a soft link and a hard link in Unix?
questions.correct_answer: A soft link is a pointer to a file name and breaks if the target is deleted; a hard link is a direct reference to the inode and persists
incorrect_answers: They are the same thing with different names
A hard link works across filesystems; a soft link does not
A soft link consumes more disk space than a hard link

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DNS resolver?
questions.correct_answer: To translate human-readable domain names into IP addresses by querying DNS servers
incorrect_answers: To assign IP addresses to devices

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
What is lazy evaluation?
questions.correct_answer: A strategy where expressions are not evaluated until their values are actually needed
incorrect_answers: Evaluating all expressions at compile time

questions.question: What is a container in cloud computing?
questions.correct_answer: A lightweight, standalone package that includes an application and all its dependencies, sharing the host OS kernel
incorrect_answers: A physical server in a data center
A virtual machine with its own OS

questions.question: What is the difference between a container and a virtual machine?
questions.correct_answer: Containers share the host OS kernel and are lighter; VMs include a full guest OS and are more isolated

questions.question: What is the time complexity of searching in a hash table on average?
What is a stored procedure in databases?
questions.correct_answer: A precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database that can be executed as a single unit
incorrect_answers: A method for encrypting data at rest
A backup procedure for the database
A design pattern for data access

questions.question: What is an API gateway?
questions.correct_answer: A server that acts as a single entry point for multiple backend services, handling routing, authentication, and rate limiting

questions.question: What is test-driven development (TDD)?
questions.correct_answer: A development practice where tests are written before the code, and the code is then written to pass those tests
incorrect_answers: Writing tests after the software is deployed
Running tests on a separate computer

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Makefile?
questions.correct_answer: To define build rules and dependencies so that a project can be compiled and linked automatically
incorrect_answers: To create new files and directories

questions.question: What is mocking in software testing?
questions.correct_answer: Replacing real dependencies with simulated objects to isolate the unit being tested
incorrect_answers: Making fun of poorly written code

questions.question: What is sharding in databases?
questions.correct_answer: Splitting a large database into smaller, more manageable pieces called shards, distributed across multiple servers
incorrect_answers: Creating backup copies of a database

questions.question: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
questions.correct_answer: Objects of a subclass should be replaceable with objects of their superclass without altering the correctness of the program
incorrect_answers: A class should have only one reason to change
Code should depend on abstractions, not concretions
Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification

questions.question: What is the Open/Closed Principle in SOLID?
questions.correct_answer: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
incorrect_answers: Each class should have one responsibility
Subclasses should be substitutable for their parents
Clients should not depend on interfaces they don't use

questions.question: What is connection pooling?
questions.correct_answer: Reusing a pool of pre-established database connections instead of opening and closing a new one for every request
incorrect_answers: Sharing a single connection across all users

questions.question: What is a coroutine?
questions.correct_answer: A function that can suspend its execution and resume later, allowing cooperative multitasking
incorrect_answers: A function that runs on a separate thread
A function that cannot be interrupted

questions.question: What is the difference between a stack overflow and a heap overflow?
questions.correct_answer: A stack overflow occurs when the call stack exceeds its limit (e.g., deep recursion); a heap overflow occurs when dynamically allocated memory is exceeded or corrupted
incorrect_answers: A heap overflow only happens in Java
A stack overflow is caused by too many global variables

questions.question: What is A/B testing in software?
questions.correct_answer: Comparing two versions of a feature by showing them to different user groups and measuring which performs better
incorrect_answers: Testing code on platform A and platform B
Running two test suites in parallel
Comparing code written by developer A and B

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting at the beginning of a singly linked list?
What is method overriding?
questions.correct_answer: Redefining a method inherited from a parent class in a subclass with a new implementation
incorrect_answers: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters
Calling a parent method from a child class
Making a method private in a subclass

questions.question: What is the purpose of Kubernetes?
questions.correct_answer: To automate the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications

questions.question: What is a service mesh?
questions.correct_answer: An infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication, providing features like load balancing, encryption, and observability

questions.question: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?
questions.correct_answer: Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare and checks at commit time; pessimistic locking acquires locks upfront to prevent conflicts
incorrect_answers: They are the same technique with different names
Optimistic locking always uses database locks
Pessimistic locking never prevents concurrent access

questions.question: What is MapReduce?
questions.correct_answer: A programming model for processing large data sets in parallel by mapping a function over data and then reducing the results

questions.question: What is a bloom filter?
questions.correct_answer: A probabilistic data structure that tests whether an element is in a set, allowing false positives but never false negatives

questions.question: What is technical debt?
questions.correct_answer: The implied cost of future rework caused by choosing a quick or easy solution now instead of a better approach
incorrect_answers: The cost of purchasing software licenses

questions.question: What is the purpose of a linter?
questions.correct_answer: To analyze source code for potential errors, bugs, and style violations without executing it
incorrect_answers: To compile code into machine language

questions.question: What is the difference between strong typing and weak typing?
questions.correct_answer: Strongly typed languages enforce strict type rules and prevent implicit conversions; weakly typed languages allow more implicit type coercion
incorrect_answers: Weakly typed languages prevent all bugs

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic typing?
questions.correct_answer: Static typing checks types at compile time; dynamic typing checks types at runtime
incorrect_answers: Static typing is slower at runtime

questions.question: What is an in-memory database?
questions.correct_answer: A database that stores data primarily in RAM for faster read and write operations
incorrect_answers: A database stored on an SSD
A database that only exists during a session
A database with no persistence whatsoever

questions.question: What is the CAP theorem's 'Partition Tolerance'?
questions.correct_answer: The system continues to operate despite communication breakdowns between network nodes
incorrect_answers: The system can partition data across tables
The system is tolerant of SQL syntax errors
The system can handle disk partitioning

questions.question: What is eventual consistency?
questions.correct_answer: A consistency model where, given enough time without new updates, all replicas will converge to the same value
incorrect_answers: Data is always immediately consistent across all nodes
Data is never consistent across replicas
Consistency is checked only once per day

questions.question: What is the purpose of a circuit breaker pattern in distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: To detect failures and prevent cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service and allowing it to recover
incorrect_answers: To protect electrical hardware from surges
To limit the number of database connections

questions.question: What is a B-tree?
questions.correct_answer: A self-balancing tree structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time, commonly used in databases and file systems
incorrect_answers: A binary tree with exactly two levels
A tree named after its inventor whose last name starts with B
A tree that stores only boolean values

questions.question: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
questions.correct_answer: Symmetric uses the same key for encryption and decryption; asymmetric uses a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption
incorrect_answers: Asymmetric uses the same key for both operations

questions.question: What is a cron job?
questions.correct_answer: A scheduled task on Unix-like systems that runs automatically at specified times or intervals
incorrect_answers: A background process that runs continuously

questions.question: What is the difference between compilation and transpilation?
questions.correct_answer: Compilation converts source code to a lower-level language (like machine code); transpilation converts source code to another language at a similar level
incorrect_answers: Transpilation produces machine code; compilation does not
Compilation only works for C programs

questions.question: What is the halting problem?
questions.correct_answer: The undecidable problem of determining whether an arbitrary program will finish running or run forever
incorrect_answers: A bug that causes a program to crash
A problem with stopping a running Docker container

questions.question: What does it mean for a problem to be NP-complete?
questions.correct_answer: It is in NP (solutions can be verified in polynomial time) and every other NP problem can be reduced to it in polynomial time
incorrect_answers: It can be solved in polynomial time
It can only be solved using quantum computers

questions.question: What is the P vs NP problem?
questions.correct_answer: The unsolved question of whether every problem whose solution can be verified quickly (NP) can also be solved quickly (P)
incorrect_answers: The question of whether parallel computing is faster
Whether Python is better than Node.js
Whether all problems have polynomial solutions

questions.question: What is a Turing machine?
questions.correct_answer: A theoretical computational model consisting of an infinite tape, a head that reads and writes symbols, and a state machine that defines transitions
incorrect_answers: An early physical computer built by Alan Turing

questions.question: What is the Church-Turing thesis?
questions.correct_answer: The hypothesis that any function computable by an effective procedure can be computed by a Turing machine
incorrect_answers: A theorem proving that all algorithms terminate
A mathematical proof about prime numbers
A conjecture about the speed of quantum computers

questions.question: What is amortized analysis?
questions.correct_answer: A method of averaging the time complexity of operations over a sequence, showing that occasional expensive operations are offset by many cheap ones
incorrect_answers: Measuring the worst-case time of a single operation
Calculating the memory usage of a program
Estimating the cost of cloud computing resources

questions.question: What is the amortized time complexity of appending to a dynamic array (like Python's list or Java's ArrayList)?
questions.correct_answer: O(1) amortized

questions.question: What is a red-black tree?
questions.correct_answer: A self-balancing binary search tree where each node has a color (red or black) and specific rules ensure the tree remains approximately balanced
incorrect_answers: A tree that stores RGB color values
A graph with alternating colored edges
A binary tree with exactly two colors of leaves

questions.question: What is the maximum height of a red-black tree with n nodes?
questions.correct_answer: 2 * log₂(n + 1)

questions.question: What is the Byzantine Generals Problem?
questions.correct_answer: A problem in distributed computing where components may fail and give conflicting information, requiring consensus despite some traitorous or faulty nodes
incorrect_answers: A problem about military strategy simulation
An optimization problem for routing in networks
A memory allocation problem in operating systems

questions.question: What is the Paxos algorithm used for?
questions.correct_answer: Achieving consensus among distributed nodes even in the presence of failures

questions.question: What is the Raft consensus algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: A consensus algorithm designed to be more understandable than Paxos, using leader election and log replication to achieve agreement among distributed nodes
incorrect_answers: A shortest path algorithm for graphs

questions.question: What is a vector clock in distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: A mechanism for tracking causality between events across distributed nodes using a vector of logical timestamps, one per node
incorrect_answers: A physical clock synchronized via NTP
A timer for rate limiting requests
A scheduling algorithm for CPU time

questions.question: What is a Merkle tree?
questions.correct_answer: A tree where every leaf contains a hash of a data block and every non-leaf contains a hash of its children, enabling efficient and secure data verification
incorrect_answers: A tree structure used only in Bitcoin mining
A trie for storing cryptographic keys

questions.question: What is consistent hashing?
questions.correct_answer: A hashing technique where adding or removing a node in a distributed system only requires remapping a fraction of the keys, rather than rehashing everything
incorrect_answers: A hash function that never produces collisions
Hashing the same key always to the same bucket in any hash table
A method for consistent database backups

questions.question: What is the two-phase commit protocol (2PC)?
questions.correct_answer: A distributed transaction protocol with a prepare phase (where participants vote) and a commit phase (where the coordinator finalizes the transaction)
incorrect_answers: A method for two users to commit to the same file simultaneously

questions.question: What is a CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type)?
questions.correct_answer: A data structure that can be replicated across multiple nodes and merged automatically without conflicts, guaranteeing eventual consistency
incorrect_answers: A conflict resolution strategy in Git

questions.question: What is the KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) algorithm used for?
questions.correct_answer: Efficient string pattern matching in O(n + m) time by using a failure function to avoid re-examining characters
incorrect_answers: Finding the shortest path in a graph

questions.question: What is the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: A string matching algorithm that uses hashing to find pattern matches in a text, with average-case O(n + m) time
incorrect_answers: A sorting algorithm for linked lists

questions.question: What is a suffix tree?
questions.correct_answer: A compressed trie of all suffixes of a string, enabling fast substring search, longest repeated substring, and other string operations
incorrect_answers: A binary tree storing file extensions
A tree where only leaf nodes store data
A balanced tree for numeric data

questions.question: What is the A* search algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: A graph search algorithm that finds the shortest path using both actual cost from the start and a heuristic estimate to the goal
incorrect_answers: An improved version of bubble sort
A brute-force search through all paths

questions.question: What is the Bellman-Ford algorithm, and how does it differ from Dijkstra's?
questions.correct_answer: Bellman-Ford finds shortest paths and can handle negative edge weights, unlike Dijkstra's, but runs in O(V*E) instead of O((V+E) log V)
incorrect_answers: Bellman-Ford is always faster than Dijkstra's
Bellman-Ford only works on unweighted graphs
Dijkstra's handles negative weights; Bellman-Ford does not

questions.question: What is the Floyd-Warshall algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: A dynamic programming algorithm that finds shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph in O(V³) time

questions.question: What is the max-flow min-cut theorem?
questions.correct_answer: In a flow network, the maximum amount of flow from source to sink equals the minimum capacity of edges that, if removed, would disconnect source from sink
incorrect_answers: The maximum flow always equals the number of edges
The minimum cut always equals the number of vertices
Maximum flow and minimum cut are unrelated

questions.question: What is the difference between Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms?
questions.correct_answer: Both find minimum spanning trees, but Kruskal's adds the shortest edge that doesn't form a cycle, while Prim's grows the MST from a starting vertex by adding the nearest neighbor
incorrect_answers: Kruskal's finds shortest paths; Prim's finds spanning trees
They produce different trees for the same graph
Prim's is always faster than Kruskal's

questions.question: What is the traveling salesman problem (TSP)?
questions.correct_answer: Finding the shortest route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city — an NP-hard optimization problem
incorrect_answers: Finding the shortest path between two cities

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the naive recursive Fibonacci implementation?
questions.correct_answer: O(2^n) — exponential

questions.question: What is cache coherence in multi-processor systems?
questions.correct_answer: The problem of ensuring that multiple CPU caches reflecting the same memory location remain consistent when one processor modifies the data
incorrect_answers: Making sure CDN caches have the same content

questions.question: What is the MESI protocol?
questions.correct_answer: A cache coherence protocol with four states: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, and Invalid, used to maintain consistency across CPU caches

questions.question: What is a lock-free data structure?
questions.correct_answer: A concurrent data structure that guarantees system-wide progress without using traditional locks, typically using atomic operations like CAS
incorrect_answers: A data structure that never needs synchronization
A data structure that prevents all race conditions

questions.question: What is the Compare-and-Swap (CAS) operation?
questions.correct_answer: An atomic instruction that compares a memory location's current value with an expected value and, if they match, replaces it with a new value
incorrect_answers: A sorting algorithm that compares and swaps adjacent elements

questions.question: What is the ABA problem in concurrent programming?
questions.correct_answer: When a CAS operation succeeds because a value changed from A to B and back to A, making the change undetectable despite intervening modifications
incorrect_answers: A deadlock between three processes named A, B, and A
A memory allocation error in C
A naming conflict between three variables

questions.question: What is the Dining Philosophers Problem?
questions.correct_answer: A classic concurrency problem illustrating synchronization challenges, where philosophers sitting around a table must share forks without deadlocking
incorrect_answers: A problem about optimizing restaurant seating
A scheduling algorithm for database queries

questions.question: What is Banker's Algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: A deadlock avoidance algorithm that checks if granting a resource request would leave the system in a safe state where all processes can finish
incorrect_answers: An algorithm for financial transaction processing
A load balancing algorithm for web servers
A scheduling algorithm for CPU processes

questions.question: What is the Readers-Writers Problem?
questions.correct_answer: A synchronization problem where multiple readers can access a resource simultaneously, but writers need exclusive access
incorrect_answers: A file permission issue in Unix
A problem with concurrent database writes
A challenge in compiling multi-file programs

questions.question: What is lambda calculus?
questions.correct_answer: A formal system in mathematical logic for expressing computation based on function abstraction and application, forming the foundation of functional programming
incorrect_answers: A calculus course about lambda functions

questions.question: What is a monad in functional programming?
questions.correct_answer: A design pattern that wraps a value in a computational context and provides operations (bind/flatMap) to chain computations while managing side effects
incorrect_answers: A data type for storing a single value
A function with exactly one parameter

questions.question: What is the Curry-Howard correspondence?
questions.correct_answer: The isomorphism between proofs in logic and programs in typed lambda calculus — types correspond to propositions, and programs correspond to proofs
incorrect_answers: A method for currying functions in Haskell
A theorem about hash function distribution
A correspondence between SQL and NoSQL databases

questions.question: What is referential transparency?
questions.correct_answer: The property where an expression can always be replaced with its value without changing the program's behavior — it produces no side effects
incorrect_answers: The ability to pass objects by reference
A property of transparent database transactions

questions.question: What is the Y combinator in lambda calculus?
questions.correct_answer: A higher-order function that enables recursion in languages that don't natively support named self-reference

questions.question: What is Amdahl's Law?
questions.correct_answer: A formula that gives the theoretical maximum speedup of a program from parallelization, limited by the sequential portion of the code
incorrect_answers: A law stating that transistor count doubles every two years

questions.question: What is the difference between RISC and CISC architectures?
questions.correct_answer: RISC uses a small set of simple, fast instructions; CISC uses a large set of complex instructions that can do more per instruction
incorrect_answers: RISC is for desktops; CISC is for mobile
There is no meaningful difference in modern CPUs

questions.question: What is speculative execution in CPUs?
questions.correct_answer: A technique where the CPU predicts the path of a branch and executes instructions ahead of time, discarding results if the prediction is wrong
incorrect_answers: Running code in a sandbox for security testing
Executing code without compiling it first
A debugging mode that guesses variable values

questions.question: What vulnerability was exposed by the Spectre and Meltdown attacks?
questions.correct_answer: They exploited speculative execution and out-of-order execution in CPUs to leak sensitive data from protected memory
incorrect_answers: They exploited SQL injection in web servers
They targeted buffer overflows in C programs

questions.question: What is the Diffie-Hellman key exchange?
questions.correct_answer: A cryptographic protocol that allows two parties to establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel without prior shared secrets
incorrect_answers: An algorithm for encrypting files at rest
A protocol for transferring files securely

questions.question: What is a zero-knowledge proof?
questions.correct_answer: A cryptographic method where one party can prove to another that a statement is true without revealing any information beyond the truth of the statement itself
incorrect_answers: A proof that a program has no bugs
A database query that returns zero results
A method of deleting all user data

questions.question: What is homomorphic encryption?
questions.correct_answer: A form of encryption that allows computations to be performed on ciphertext, producing an encrypted result that, when decrypted, matches the result of operations on the plaintext
incorrect_answers: Encryption that uses the same key for all data
Encryption that only works on numeric data

questions.question: What is the RSA algorithm based on?
questions.correct_answer: The practical difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers
incorrect_answers: The difficulty of computing square roots
The complexity of sorting large arrays
The difficulty of finding shortest paths in graphs

questions.question: What is thrashing in operating systems?
questions.correct_answer: A condition where the system spends more time swapping pages between RAM and disk than executing actual work, causing severe performance degradation
incorrect_answers: An infinite loop in a user program

questions.question: What is the working set model in memory management?
questions.correct_answer: A model that defines the set of pages a process is actively using within a given time window, used to prevent thrashing
incorrect_answers: The total amount of RAM in a system
The set of files open in an IDE
The number of active database connections

questions.question: What is the LRU (Least Recently Used) page replacement algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: An algorithm that evicts the page that has not been accessed for the longest time when a page fault occurs and memory is full
incorrect_answers: An algorithm that removes the most recently used page
An algorithm that randomly selects a page to evict
An algorithm that evicts the smallest page first

questions.question: What is Bélády's anomaly?
questions.correct_answer: The counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can actually increase the number of page faults with certain page replacement algorithms like FIFO
incorrect_answers: A bug in the LRU algorithm
A problem with cache coherence in multi-core CPUs

questions.question: What is a skip list?
questions.correct_answer: A probabilistic data structure that uses multiple layers of linked lists with increasing step sizes, allowing O(log n) average search, insertion, and deletion
incorrect_answers: A linked list that skips every other element
An array that ignores null values
A queue that skips duplicate elements

questions.question: What is the difference between strong and weak consistency in distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: Strong consistency ensures every read returns the most recent write; weak consistency allows stale reads temporarily
incorrect_answers: Strong consistency is always slower and never used
Weak consistency prevents all data loss
They are the same under the CAP theorem

questions.question: What is a quorum in distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: The minimum number of nodes that must agree on an operation for it to be considered successful, typically a majority
incorrect_answers: The total number of nodes in the cluster
The maximum latency allowed for a request

questions.question: What is the CAP theorem's practical implication for database choice?
questions.correct_answer: In the presence of network partitions, a system must choose between consistency and availability — you cannot have both
incorrect_answers: You can always have consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
Partition tolerance is optional in modern systems
Only SQL databases face CAP limitations

questions.question: What is instruction pipelining in CPUs?
questions.correct_answer: A technique where multiple instruction stages (fetch, decode, execute, etc.) overlap so different instructions are in different stages simultaneously
incorrect_answers: A method for sending instructions over a network
Storing all instructions in cache before execution

questions.question: What is a pipeline hazard?
questions.correct_answer: A situation in CPU pipelining where the next instruction cannot execute in the next clock cycle due to data dependencies, control flow changes, or resource conflicts
incorrect_answers: A physical danger from overheating CPUs
An error in the software build pipeline
A network latency spike in a CI/CD pipeline

questions.question: What is branch prediction?
questions.correct_answer: A CPU optimization technique that guesses the direction of a conditional branch before the condition is evaluated, to keep the pipeline full
incorrect_answers: Predicting which Git branch will be merged next
A compiler optimization that removes unused branches
A testing technique for code branches

questions.question: What is the difference between preemptive and cooperative multitasking?
questions.correct_answer: In preemptive multitasking, the OS can forcibly switch tasks; in cooperative multitasking, tasks must voluntarily yield control
incorrect_answers: They are identical in modern operating systems

questions.question: What is a memory barrier (fence)?
questions.correct_answer: A CPU instruction that enforces an ordering constraint on memory operations, preventing the compiler and CPU from reordering reads and writes across the barrier
incorrect_answers: A hardware device that limits RAM access

questions.question: What is the SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) problem?
questions.correct_answer: The problem of determining whether there exists an assignment of variables that makes a given boolean formula true — the first problem proven NP-complete
incorrect_answers: A method for satisfying database constraints

questions.question: What is the Cook-Levin theorem?
questions.correct_answer: The theorem that proves the Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete, establishing the foundation for the theory of NP-completeness
incorrect_answers: A theorem about cooking optimal algorithms
A theorem proving P equals NP

questions.question: What is the difference between NP-hard and NP-complete?
questions.correct_answer: NP-complete problems are both in NP and NP-hard; NP-hard problems are at least as hard as NP problems but may not be in NP themselves
incorrect_answers: NP-complete problems cannot be verified in polynomial time
Every NP-hard problem is also NP-complete

questions.question: What is a write-ahead log (WAL) in databases?
questions.correct_answer: A technique where changes are written to a log before being applied to the database, ensuring durability and crash recovery
incorrect_answers: A log of all read operations
A backup file created after each write
A debug log for slow queries

questions.question: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control in databases?
questions.correct_answer: Optimistic assumes conflicts are rare and validates at commit; pessimistic acquires locks before accessing data to prevent conflicts

questions.question: What is MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)?
questions.correct_answer: A concurrency control method where the database keeps multiple versions of data, allowing readers to access a consistent snapshot without blocking writers
incorrect_answers: A version control system for databases
A method for versioning API endpoints
A technique for multiple video codec compression

questions.question: What is tail call optimization?
questions.correct_answer: A compiler optimization that reuses the current function's stack frame for a tail-recursive call, preventing stack overflow from deep recursion
incorrect_answers: Optimizing the last line of every function
Removing unused function calls at compile time
A technique for speeding up loop iteration

questions.question: What is the difference between a compiler's front end and back end?
questions.correct_answer: The front end handles lexical analysis, parsing, and semantic analysis; the back end handles code optimization and target code generation
incorrect_answers: The front end generates machine code; the back end parses source code
The front end runs on the client; the back end runs on the server
There is no distinction in modern compilers

questions.question: What is SSA (Static Single Assignment) form in compilers?
questions.correct_answer: An intermediate representation where each variable is assigned exactly once, using phi functions at control flow merge points, simplifying many optimizations
incorrect_answers: A type of single sign-on system
A method for assigning static IP addresses

questions.question: What is register allocation in compiler design?
questions.correct_answer: The process of assigning program variables to CPU registers, minimizing memory access and optimizing performance

questions.question: What is the graph coloring problem and how does it relate to register allocation?
questions.correct_answer: Graph coloring assigns colors to vertices so no adjacent vertices share a color; in register allocation, variables are vertices, interferences are edges, and colors are registers
incorrect_answers: Graph coloring is used to optimize database queries
It determines the color scheme of a GUI
It has no relation to register allocation

questions.question: What is the convex hull problem?
questions.correct_answer: Finding the smallest convex polygon that contains all points in a given set of 2D points
incorrect_answers: Finding the largest circle inside a polygon
Sorting points by distance from the origin
Finding the minimum spanning tree of points

questions.question: What is the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) used for?
questions.correct_answer: Efficiently computing the Discrete Fourier Transform, reducing the complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n), used in signal processing and polynomial multiplication
incorrect_answers: Sorting arrays of floating point numbers

questions.question: What is Shannon entropy?
questions.correct_answer: A measure of the average information or uncertainty in a random variable, used as the theoretical limit for lossless data compression
incorrect_answers: The disorder in a computer's memory
The rate of data transfer on a network
The randomness of a hash function

questions.question: What is the pigeonhole principle and how is it used in computer science?
questions.correct_answer: If n items are placed into m containers where n > m, at least one container must have more than one item — used to prove hash collisions are inevitable and in complexity proofs
incorrect_answers: A sorting algorithm for small datasets

questions.question: What is a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) and where is it commonly used?
questions.correct_answer: A directed graph with no cycles, commonly used in build systems, dependency resolution, version control (Git commits), and scheduling
incorrect_answers: A graph that must have cycles
A type of database table relationship

questions.question: What is the master theorem used for?
questions.correct_answer: Solving recurrence relations of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), commonly arising from divide-and-conquer algorithms
incorrect_answers: Proving the correctness of sorting algorithms

questions.question: What is the difference between a deterministic and a non-deterministic finite automaton (DFA vs NFA)?
questions.correct_answer: A DFA has exactly one transition for each state-input pair; an NFA can have multiple transitions or none, and can use epsilon transitions
incorrect_answers: A DFA is always slower than an NFA
An NFA requires more memory than a DFA
There is no practical difference between them

questions.question: What is Chomsky's hierarchy of formal languages?
questions.correct_answer: A classification of formal grammars into four types: Type 0 (unrestricted), Type 1 (context-sensitive), Type 2 (context-free), and Type 3 (regular), each with decreasing expressive power
incorrect_answers: A ranking of programming languages by popularity
A theory about natural language processing accuracy
A hierarchy of compiler optimization levels

questions.question: What is the pumping lemma used for?
questions.correct_answer: To prove that a language is NOT regular (or context-free), by showing it doesn't satisfy the necessary properties of regular (or context-free) languages
incorrect_answers: To prove a language is regular

questions.question: What is a context-free grammar (CFG)?
questions.correct_answer: A formal grammar where every production rule has a single non-terminal on the left side, commonly used to define programming language syntax
incorrect_answers: A grammar that requires no context to understand
A grammar used only for natural languages
A grammar with no production rules

questions.question: What is Rice's theorem?
questions.correct_answer: Any non-trivial semantic property of programs is undecidable — you cannot write a general algorithm to determine whether an arbitrary program has a given property
incorrect_answers: A theorem about rice sorting algorithms
A theorem proving all programs eventually terminate
A theorem about optimal data compression

questions.question: What is the time complexity of matrix multiplication using Strassen's algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: O(n^2.807), improving on the naive O(n³) algorithm

questions.question: What is copy-on-write (COW)?
questions.correct_answer: An optimization strategy where copies of a resource share the same underlying data until one copy is modified, at which point a true copy is made
incorrect_answers: A method that prevents any copying of data
A write operation that duplicates the entire database
A file system that only allows writing once

questions.question: What is the difference between hard real-time and soft real-time systems?
questions.correct_answer: Hard real-time systems must meet deadlines absolutely or the system fails; soft real-time systems can tolerate occasional missed deadlines with degraded performance
incorrect_answers: Hard real-time uses hardware; soft real-time uses software
There is no difference between them

questions.question: What is an epsilon transition in finite automata?
questions.correct_answer: A state transition that occurs without consuming any input symbol, allowing the automaton to change state spontaneously
incorrect_answers: A transition that consumes all remaining input
A transition that resets the automaton
A transition that only occurs on numeric input

questions.question: What is the space complexity of merge sort?
questions.correct_answer: O(n) — it requires additional space proportional to the input size for merging

questions.question: What is a wait-free algorithm?
questions.correct_answer: A concurrent algorithm where every operation is guaranteed to complete in a bounded number of steps, regardless of contention from other threads
incorrect_answers: An algorithm with no waiting loops
An algorithm that blocks until resources are available
An algorithm optimized for single-threaded execution

questions.question: What is the difference between linearizability and serializability?
questions.correct_answer: Linearizability is a real-time ordering guarantee for concurrent operations; serializability ensures transactions appear to execute sequentially, without requiring real-time ordering
